content.text: Telemetry data is only as useful as the schema behind it. Without enforced, versioned schemas, a single renaming of a span attribute cascades into broken dashboards, silenced alerts, and ETL pipelines that silently drop data.
Telemetry schema governance

is the discipline of treating trace spans, metrics, and log field definitions as versioned, reviewed artifacts — with the same rigour applied to API contracts — so that producers and consumers of observability data stay in sync across your entire agentic AI platform.
What It Is

A telemetry schema is a formal definition of the shape of a signal: which attributes a span carries, what types they are, which are required vs optional, and what enumerated values are valid. Schema governance is the process of authoring, reviewing, versioning, publishing, and retiring those definitions — and of ensuring every producer and consumer of telemetry is aligned on the same version at all times.
In OpenTelemetry terms, this maps to the
Schema URL
mechanism: every OTLP resource, span, and metric carries a
schemaUrl
field that identifies the exact schema version in use. Schema governance operationalises this field by pairing it with a registry, a review workflow, and automated compatibility enforcement.
Why It Matters

Agentic AI platforms emit telemetry from many layers simultaneously: the orchestration framework, individual tool call spans, LLM provider responses, embedding lookups, and guardrail checks. Each layer may be updated independently. Without governance, a rename of a span attribute breaks every PromQL query and Grafana panel that references the old name; a removed required field causes downstream ETL to fail silently; inconsistent enum values prevent accurate aggregation. On-call engineers cannot trust telemetry that may have drifted from the dashboards written against an older schema version.
Benefits
Dashboards and alert rules remain valid across platform upgrades because schema changes are backward-compatible or have a documented migration plan.
Consumer impact analysis identifies every dashboard, alert, and ETL job affected before a schema change ships.
Automated CI gates block breaking changes from merging without a migration plan.
Schema registry gives all teams a single source of truth for what telemetry to emit and what to expect.
OTel Schema URL pinning means each component declares exactly which version it conforms to, enabling automated compatibility checks at deploy time.
Trade-offs
Schema review adds friction to telemetry changes. Teams accustomed to ad-hoc attribute naming must adopt a pull-request workflow for schema files.
Dual-write periods during breaking migrations temporarily double storage costs.
A schema registry is infrastructure that must be operated, backed up, and kept highly available. It becomes a dependency for CI pipelines and SDK validation.
Strict versioning can slow experimentation: even a trivial debug attribute requires a schema change proposal through the review process.
How It Works

Schema governance forms a pipeline from authoring to consumption. An engineer who wants to change a span or metric modifies a schema file and opens a pull request. CI runs automated checks: schema linting, backward and forward compatibility analysis, and consumer impact scanning. If the change is breaking, CI requires a migration plan document before the PR can merge. On merge, the schema is published to the registry under a new semantic version. Every producer tags its telemetry with the updated Schema URL so consumers can verify alignment. Consumers fetch the schema from the registry at startup and validate incoming telemetry against it.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Telemetry schema governance pipeline showing flow from schema author through PR review, compatibility check, schema registry, and OTel SchemaURL, distributing to trace collectors, dashboards, alert rules, data pipelines, and SDK instrumentation

content.text: The pipeline flows left-to-right. A schema author edits a YAML file and opens a pull request. CI runs schema-lint, backward and forward compatibility checks, and a consumer impact report. Human reviewers from the Telemetry Steward group approve the PR. On merge, the schema is published to the registry with a new version and the OTel Schema URL is updated. The registry distributes the schema to all consumers: trace collectors, dashboards, alert rules, data pipelines, and SDK instrumentation libraries. Breaking changes additionally require a migration plan and a dual-write period before the old schema version is retired.
When to Use It
When multiple teams emit telemetry from different services and consume each other's signals.
When dashboards and alerts are maintained by a different team than the one emitting the spans.
When telemetry feeds production data warehouses or billing systems where schema changes have compliance implications.
When following OTel semantic conventions and you need a repeatable process for adopting new convention versions.
For any agentic platform at scale where span definitions like gen_ai.request.model or agent.tool.name must be agreed upon across all SDK users.
How to Implement
Step 1 — Define Your Schema Format

Adopt a machine-readable format for schema files. YAML is idiomatic for OTel-aligned schemas. Keep one file per signal domain (traces, metrics, logs) and document each attribute's name, type, description, and whether it is required.

# schemas/traces/agent-session.yaml
schema_version: "1.3.0"
schema_url: "https://telemetry.example.com/schemas/traces/agent-session/1.3.0"
domain: traces
description: >
  Spans emitted by the agentic orchestration layer for each user session.

spans:
  - name: agent.session
    description: Root span for one complete agent session.
    required_attributes:
      - name: agent.session_id
        type: string
        description: Unique identifier for the session.
      - name: agent.name
        type: string
        description: Logical name of the agent (e.g. "support-agent").
      - name: agent.version
        type: string
        description: Deployed version string of the agent.
      - name: gen_ai.request.model
        type: string
        description: Model identifier as returned by the LLM provider.
    optional_attributes:
      - name: agent.user_id
        type: string
        description: Hashed user identifier for session correlation.
      - name: agent.tenant_id
        type: string
        description: Tenant identifier for multi-tenant deployments.
    events:
      - name: agent.tool_call
        attributes:
          - tool.name: string (required)
          - tool.input_tokens: int
          - tool.duration_ms: double

  - name: agent.llm_call
    description: Child span for each LLM inference call within a session.
    required_attributes:
      - name: gen_ai.request.model
        type: string
      - name: gen_ai.usage.input_tokens
        type: int
      - name: gen_ai.usage.output_tokens
        type: int
    optional_attributes:
      - name: gen_ai.response.finish_reason
        type: string
        enum: [stop, length, tool_calls, content_filter]
Step 2 — Set Up a Schema Registry

The schema registry is a versioned store. For OTel-based systems, a Git-backed registry with semantic versioning and a published HTTP endpoint is sufficient. Keep old schema versions accessible under their original URLs so slow-moving consumers are not broken when a new version ships.

# Directory structure for a Git-backed schema registry
# schemas/
#   traces/
#     agent-session.yaml              <- current working draft
#     agent-session.v1.2.0.yaml       <- pinned snapshot for consumers on 1.2.0
#   metrics/
#     agent-metrics.yaml
#   logs/
#     agent-logs.yaml
#   migrations/
#     2.0.0-rename-agent-name.md      <- required for every breaking change
# CHANGELOG.md
# VERSION                             <- 1.3.0

# Tag and publish after merge
git tag schemas/traces/agent-session/v1.3.0
git push origin schemas/traces/agent-session/v1.3.0

# Consumers fetch via schema URL
curl https://telemetry.example.com/schemas/traces/agent-session/1.3.0

# OTel SDK config — pin the schema URL
otel_schema_url = "https://telemetry.example.com/schemas/traces/agent-session/1.3.0"
Step 3 — CI Compatibility Checker

The CI pipeline must classify every schema change as breaking or non-breaking and block breaking changes without a migration plan. The following script implements a structural diff for the YAML schema format.
Step 4 — GitHub Actions CI Pipeline

# .github/workflows/schema-ci.yml
name: schema-ci
on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'schemas/**'

jobs:
  schema-lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with: {fetch-depth: 0}

      - name: Install yamale
        run: pip install yamale pyyaml

      - name: Lint schema YAML structure
        run: |
          for f in schemas/**/*.yaml; do
            python -c "import yaml; yaml.safe_load(open('$f'))" || exit 1
          done

      - name: Fetch base schema versions
        run: |
          git fetch origin main
          git show origin/main:schemas/traces/agent-session.yaml \
            > /tmp/old_agent_session.yaml

      - name: Compatibility check
        run: |
          python scripts/schema_compat_check.py \
            /tmp/old_agent_session.yaml \
            schemas/traces/agent-session.yaml

      - name: Require migration plan for breaking changes
        run: |
          NEW_VER=$(grep 'schema_version' schemas/traces/agent-session.yaml \
            | awk '{print $2}' | tr -d '"')
          MAJOR=$(echo $NEW_VER | cut -d. -f1)
          OLD_VER=$(grep 'schema_version' /tmp/old_agent_session.yaml \
            | awk '{print $2}' | tr -d '"')
          OLD_MAJOR=$(echo $OLD_VER | cut -d. -f1)
          if [ "$MAJOR" -gt "$OLD_MAJOR" ]; then
            ls schemas/migrations/${NEW_VER}-*.md || \
              (echo 'MISSING migration plan for major version bump' && exit 1)
          fi

      - name: Consumer impact report
        run: python scripts/consumer_impact.py schemas/traces/agent-session.yaml
Real-World Example

OpenTelemetry's own semantic conventions are the largest production example of telemetry schema governance. The OTel specification repository manages conventions for dozens of domains using YAML files under
model/
. Every attribute has a required stability level (experimental, stable, deprecated). Breaking changes to stable attributes require a Schema URL version bump and a transition period. The
gen_ai.*
semantic conventions — used by all compliant agentic AI instrumentation libraries — went through this process:
gen_ai.request.model
was introduced as experimental, stabilised in 1.26.0, and any future rename would require a migration plan covering every SDK and dashboard that references it.
For an internal agentic platform, the same approach applies at smaller scale: define
agent.*
attributes in YAML files in a
telemetry-schemas
Git repository, run the compatibility checker in CI on every PR, and require approval from a Telemetry Steward group (typically two senior engineers) before merging changes to stable schemas.
Common Mistakes
Treating schema versioning as optional until a breaking change has already caused a production incident. Schema governance is most valuable when started before the first cross-team consumer.
Using calendar dates as schema versions (e.g., v20240301) rather than semantic versioning. SemVer communicates compatibility intent unambiguously to consumers.
Skipping the dual-write period and cutting over all producers and consumers simultaneously. This is a high-risk coordinated deployment that rarely executes cleanly across many teams.
Defining schemas only for trace spans and ignoring metrics. Metric renames (e.g., agent_requests to agent_requests_total) are equally breaking for Prometheus alert rules and recording rules.
Not maintaining a consumer registry mapping each schema field to the dashboards, alert rules, and pipelines that depend on it. Without it, consumer impact analysis is manual and incomplete.
Allowing any engineer to merge schema changes without review. A Telemetry Steward group — even two people with CODEOWNERS enforcement — prevents casual breakage of production monitoring.
Best Practices
Follow OTel semantic conventions for all gen_ai.* and agent.* attributes before inventing custom names. Alignment with community standards reduces the scope of custom governance.
Use semantic versioning: major for breaking changes, minor for new optional fields, patch for documentation fixes only.
Pin the Schema URL in every SDK config, collector config, and instrumentation library so version mismatches surface at startup rather than silently producing malformed telemetry.
Require a migration plan document for every major version bump before CI passes. The plan must list affected consumers, the dual-write window duration, and explicit cut-over steps.
Keep old schema URL endpoints alive for at least one full release cycle after deprecation so consumers that update slowly are not immediately broken.
Document expected cardinality for each attribute in the schema file. Schema review is the correct place to catch high-cardinality label candidates before they reach Prometheus.
Performance & Scalability

Schema files are small (typically under 100 KB), so the registry itself has negligible storage and latency requirements. CI schema diffs run in seconds. The expensive part of governance at scale is consumer impact scanning: searching all dashboards, alert rule files, and ETL configs for references to changed field names. Cache this scan and invalidate only when consumer files change, not on every schema PR. For large platforms with hundreds of dashboards, a field-reference index (a JSON map from field name to list of consumer files) makes impact analysis a lookup rather than a filesystem search.
Tooling & Ecosystem

OpenTelemetry Weaver — the official OTel CLI for validating and generating code from semantic convention YAML files. Supports schema compatibility checking and template-based SDK generation from semconv models.
Confluent Schema Registry — production-grade registry for Avro, Protobuf, and JSON Schema with built-in BACKWARD, FORWARD, and FULL compatibility modes. Useful when telemetry flows through Kafka.
Apicurio Registry — open-source alternative to Confluent Schema Registry with a REST API compatible interface.
buf CLI — originally for Protobuf breaking-change detection; the compatibility rules and migration workflow are instructive for any schema governance system.
OTel Schema File Format — the official spec at opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/otel/schemas/ defines the attribute renaming and transformation format used in Schema URL resolution.
Comparison with Alternatives

Ad-hoc attribute naming
None

Security & Reliability
Sign published schemas with a cryptographic signature so consumers can verify they received the canonical version and not an injected schema from a compromised registry.
Control who can approve schema changes via CODEOWNERS or a dedicated Telemetry Steward team. Breaking changes to stable attributes require senior review.
Treat the schema registry as high-availability infrastructure. It is accessed by CI pipelines and SDK startup validation; outages can block deployments.

Avoid storing PII in schema field descriptions. Schema files may document that a user_id field exists without containing actual values, but certain field names can reveal product features. Keep internal schemas in private registries.
Mental Model
Think of telemetry schemas as the
API contract

between the teams that produce observability data and the teams that consume it. Just as you would not rename a REST API field without a versioned migration path, you should not rename a span attribute without one. The schema registry is the
API documentation and endpoint catalogue
for this contract. The OTel Schema URL is the
Accept-Version header

that producers stamp on every message so consumers know exactly which contract version is in use. Backward compatibility means old consumers work with new producers. Forward compatibility means new consumers can still read old archived telemetry — essential for data warehouse queries that span multiple schema generations.
Key Takeaways
Telemetry schemas are contracts. Treat them as versioned API contracts between observability producers and consumers.
Use semantic versioning: major for breaking changes, minor for new optional fields, patch for documentation only.
Automate compatibility checking in CI so breaking changes require a migration plan before they can merge.
Run consumer impact analysis before any breaking change to know the full scope of dashboard, alert rule, and pipeline updates required.
Use dual-write periods for breaking changes: emit both old and new attribute names simultaneously while consumers migrate at their own pace.
Pin OTel Schema URLs in every SDK and collector config so version mismatches are detected at startup rather than silently tolerated.
Follow OTel gen_ai semantic conventions for LLM and agent attributes before inventing custom names.
